headline: How Retail Investors are navigating volatility
summary: As global markets face geopolitical uncertainty and a shifting interest rate environment, traders are evolving. But how are different generations of Wall Street approaching these chaotic times? This episode of Trader Talk dives into the retail investor game, exploring how newer traders are approaching these markets. Host Kenny Polcari is joined by Robinhood CIO Stephanie Guild, Yahoo Finance Anchor Julie Hyman, and Former Head of People Ops at Ellevest, Amanda Polcari, to discuss.

headline: SEC Approves Plan Removing Day-Trading Limit for Investors
summary: (Bloomberg) -- The US Securities and Exchange Commission gave the go-ahead for sweeping changes to a restriction on day-trading activity by small investors on Tuesday in a move cheered by retail brokers. The Financial Industry Regulatory Authority, Wall Street’s self-regulatory watchdog, had proposed reworking the pattern day trading rule, which bans a trader from making more than four day-trades in a five-day period if their margin account has less than $25,000 in assets.The new margin standard

headline: End of ‘Pattern Day Trader’ Rule Could Turbocharge Retail Stock Trading
summary: A Securities and Exchange Commission move to axe a decades-old rule aimed at damping risky trades could encourage small investors to get even more active in the U.S. stock market.  Retail brokerages such as Robinhood Markets are among the likely beneficiaries of the SEC’s elimination of the “pattern day trader” rule, a long-awaited decision announced in a regulatory notice yesterday.  Shares of Robinhood and its rival Webull both ended the day more than 10% higher and are climbing further ahead of today’s opening bell.

content.title: End of ‘Pattern Day Trader’ Rule Could Turbocharge Retail Stock Trading
content.summary: A Securities and Exchange Commission move to axe a decades-old rule aimed at damping risky trades could encourage small investors to get even more active in the U.S. stock market.  Retail brokerages such as Robinhood Markets are among the likely beneficiaries of the SEC’s elimination of the “pattern day trader” rule, a long-awaited decision announced in a regulatory notice yesterday.  Shares of Robinhood and its rival Webull both ended the day more than 10% higher and are climbing further ahead of today’s opening bell.

headline: Why Robinhood Stock Is Surging Even as Bitcoin Price Falls
summary: Robinhood  shares were rising ahead of the bell Wednesday after the Securities and Exchange Commission approved some important changes for the cryptocurrency exchange.  Robinhood  gained 3.8% in premarket trading—having climbed over 10% the previous session—following the SEC’s Tuesday announcement to extend retail investors’ ability to use borrowed money to invest.  Under the old rules, margin account holders who made four or more day-trades within five business days had to keep at least $25,000 in their accounts, effectively shutting out smaller traders.

content.title: Why Robinhood Stock Is Surging Even as Bitcoin Price Falls

Robinhood  gained 3.8% in premarket trading—having climbed over 10% the previous session—following the SEC’s Tuesday announcement to extend retail investors’ ability to use borrowed money to invest.  Under the old rules, margin account holders who made four or more day-trades within five business days had to keep at least $25,000 in their accounts, effectively shutting out smaller traders.

headline: Crypto stocks rally on renewed hopes of US-Iran peace talks
summary: Cryptocurrency bitcoin (BTC-USD) is holding on to stay above $74,000 per token on Wednesday. Yahoo Finance Markets and Data Editor Jared Blikre takes a closer look at the crypto market and the gains that crypto-related stocks are showing this morning on the back of renewed hopes that the US and Iran can agree to a peace deal.

headline: Retail Investors Keep Getting Burned in the Stock Market. They’re Piling In Again Now.
summary: Wall Street’s ambivalence toward retail investors was summed up by Joseph P. Kennedy Sr., explaining what convinced him to get out of the stock market before the Crash of 1929.  It was, he said, unsolicited investing advice from the guy polishing his wingtips.  “When the shoeshine boys have tips, the stock market is too popular for its own good,” said Kennedy, who sold short and made a fortune, giving rise to an American political dynasty.

content.title: Retail Investors Keep Getting Burned in the Stock Market. They’re Piling In Again Now.
content.summary: Wall Street’s ambivalence toward retail investors was summed up by Joseph P. Kennedy Sr., explaining what convinced him to get out of the stock market before the Crash of 1929.
